CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized and presents its contributions and methodology (pointer network + actor-critic RL, Active Search) clearly early on, with datasets, baselines, metrics and running-time reported. Notation and experimental details appear well specified (hyperparameters reported, thorough related-work coverage), and limitations are explicitly discussed. Figures/tables and examples seem informative given the detailed comparative results, and the writing quality is described as good, suggesting only minor presentation issues remain.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims are backed by empirical evidence: RL-trained pointer networks outperform supervised pointers and classical heuristics on 2D Euclidean TSP (n up to 100) and generalize to knapsack (optimal up to 200 items), with detailed baselines (Christofides, Concorde, LK-H, OR-Tools, etc.), reported hyperparameters and available code. The methodological core (actor-critic policy-gradient training of a pointer network and instance-level Active Search) is standard and appears correctly applied; no incorrect mathematical derivations are indicated in the summary. Experimental methodology is generally sound given the wide range of baselines and datasets, though Active Search’s extreme runtime and occasional steering to suboptimal regions are acknowledged limitations that slightly weaken practical claims. Overall the paper is technically solid but has minor issues in scalability and computational cost that do not invalidate the main conclusions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper introduces a novel application of reinforcement learning to pointer networks and proposes Active Search (on-line policy-gradient optimization per instance), which represents a clear new method/perspective for combinatorial optimization. The underlying problems (TSP, 0-1 Knapsack) are well-studied, but the combination of actor-critic policy-gradient training of a pointer network and per-instance Active Search is a non-trivial extension beyond prior supervised pointer-network work and classical heuristics. The authors explicitly differentiate their RL pretraining vs supervised baselines and demonstrate empirical gains (e.g., outperforming supervised pointer networks and classical heuristics on 2D Euclidean TSP up to 100 nodes), so the contribution would inform experts while not redefining the problem itself.

main_claims: Introduce Neural Combinatorial Optimization, a framework that uses neural networks and reinforcement learning to tackle combinatorial optimization problems.

Propose two training/inference approaches: RL pretraining (actor-critic policy-gradient training of a pointer network) and Active Search (on-line policy-gradient optimization on a single test instance), and show their combination works best.

Demonstrate that the RL-trained pointer network significantly outperforms supervised pointer networks and classical heuristics (e.g., Christofides) on 2D Euclidean TSP up to 100 nodes, and obtains close-to-optimal results with additional search.
Show the framework generalizes to other problems (0-1 Knapsack) and achieves optimal results on instances with up to 200 items.
_summary.methodology: Use a pointer network (encoder-decoder LSTMs with an attention-based pointing mechanism) parameterizing a stochastic policy over tours, trained with policy-gradient RL (actor-critic) to minimize expected tour length; at inference, obtain solutions via sampling, greedy decoding, or Active Search that updates model parameters on a single instance.
limitations: Method is still inferior to state-of-the-art solvers in speed, scale and performance (explicitly stated).
Active Search can be very time-consuming (reported ~7 hours per TSP50 instance and ~25 hours per TSP100 instance in experiments).
Supervised pointer networks generalize poorly compared to the RL approach (explicitly reported).
Generalization of RL pretraining depends on the training data distribution; Active Search is distribution-independent.
Feasibility of solutions for problems with complex constraints can be challenging; may require problem-specific handling or penalty terms (stated).
For some cases (TSP50) Active Search can steer search toward suboptimal regions (observed).
_summary.topic_area: Combinatorial optimization — TSP and Knapsack using neural networks and reinforcement learning
